content.text: Every AI outage affects your customers differently depending on their contract tier, the type of incident, and whether they actually experienced the impact. Sending the same template to everyone wastes goodwill with customers who weren't affected and under-serves enterprise customers who need personalised detail. This topic provides a complete set of templates for each incident type and customer tier, along with the decision logic for choosing which template to send and when.

attrs.alt: Decision tree for customer communications showing branching logic: directly impacted yes/no, safety incident yes/no, enterprise or self-serve, leading to legal review, personalised email, or status page plus broadcast email

content.text: Communication Decision Logic
Before selecting a template, answer these three questions in order:
Question
Yes
No
Is the customer directly impacted?
Proceed to Q2
Status page only — no direct outreach
Is it a safety incident?
Stop — Legal reviews all comms before sending
Proceed to Q3
Is the customer on an enterprise contract?
Personalised email at T+30, plus dedicated Slack if available
Broadcast email to all affected self-serve users at T+60

For safety incidents, the sequence is: IC briefs Legal at T+15 → Legal approves comms language → Comms Liaison sends approved template → no improvisation. Any template used for a safety incident must be approved by Legal before sending.
Template 1: Enterprise Customer — Declaration (SEV1 / SEV2)

Send this to enterprise technical contacts within 30 minutes of declaring a SEV1 or within 60 minutes for a SEV2, only if the customer is confirmed to be impacted. The CSM (Customer Success Manager) should be CC'd.

To: {customer_technical_contact}
CC: {csm_email}, incidents@yourcompany.com
From: incidents@yourcompany.com
Subject: [SEV1] Service Disruption — {Your Company} Inference API — {date}

Dear {customer_name} team,

We are writing to notify you of an active incident affecting the {Your Company} Inference API.

Incident summary:
  Service affected: Inference API
  Impact: Elevated latency and errors on inference requests
  Start time: {start_time} UTC
  Affected region: {region}
  Your impact: Based on your usage, you may be seeing {user-facing symptom}

What we are doing:
Our engineering team declared this a SEV1 at {start_time} UTC and is actively
working to restore full service. We have identified the affected component and
are implementing a mitigation.

Next steps:
  - We will email you again by {next_update_time} UTC with a full status update
  - Live status is available at: https://status.yourcompany.com
  - Your dedicated support channel: {slack_channel_link} (if applicable)

We apologise for the disruption to your service and will keep you informed
throughout the incident.

— {Your Company} Incident Response Team
  On behalf of IC: {ic_name}
Template 2: Enterprise Customer — Update

Send this every 60 minutes to enterprise customers for an ongoing SEV1, or on significant status changes (root cause identified, mitigation deployed). Internal updates are more frequent — this is external-facing and longer-spaced.

Subject: [UPDATE] {Your Company} Inference API Incident — {date} {time} UTC

Dear {customer_name} team,

This is an update on the active Inference API incident.

Current status: {Investigating / Identified / Monitoring}
Duration so far: {N} minutes / hours

What we know:
{2-3 sentences on current understanding. No speculation. No internal system names.}

What we are doing:
{1-2 sentences on current actions.}

What you can do:
{Optional: if a workaround is available, describe it here.
 E.g. 'You may route requests to the EU region as an interim workaround.'}

Next update: {next_update_time} UTC, or sooner if the situation changes significantly.
Live status: https://status.yourcompany.com

— {Your Company} Incident Response Team
Template 3: Enterprise Customer — Resolution
Send this as soon as the incident is resolved. This is the most important external communication — it closes the loop and is often the one that gets read most carefully by enterprise customers.

Subject: [RESOLVED] {Your Company} Inference API Incident — {date}

Dear {customer_name} team,

We are pleased to confirm that the Inference API has been fully restored.

Incident summary:
  Start: {start_time} UTC
  Resolution: {resolution_time} UTC
  Duration: {N} hours {M} minutes
  Your impact: {description of what the customer experienced}

Root cause:
{One paragraph in plain language. No internal system names. Factual and specific.}

What we are doing to prevent recurrence:
  1. {Corrective action 1}
  2. {Corrective action 2}
  3. {Corrective action 3}

Post-incident report:
A full post-incident report will be available within five business days at
https://status.yourcompany.com/history

We understand the impact this incident had on your operations and sincerely
apologise for the disruption.

If you have any questions or would like to discuss the incident further,
please reply to this email or reach out to your Customer Success Manager,
{csm_name} at {csm_email}.

— {Your Company} Incident Response Team
Template 4: Self-Serve Customers — Broadcast Email

For self-serve (free tier, pay-as-you-go) customers, personalised outreach is not scalable. Use a broadcast email to all users who had API activity in the affected region during the incident window. Send at resolution or within 24 hours for a resolved SEV1.

Subject: {Your Company} Inference API — Service Disruption on {date}

We want to let you know about a service disruption that may have affected your
use of the {Your Company} Inference API on {date}.

What happened:
Between {start_time} UTC and {resolution_time} UTC ({N} minutes), some Inference
API requests in the {region} region experienced elevated latency and errors.

Were you affected?
If you made Inference API calls during this window and noticed errors or slow
responses, this incident may have been the cause. You can review your usage
and any failed requests in the {Your Company} dashboard:
https://app.yourcompany.com/usage

What we did:
{1-2 sentences on mitigation, plain language.}

Full details and a post-incident report are available at:
https://status.yourcompany.com/incidents/{incident-id}

We apologise for any disruption this caused to your projects.

— The {Your Company} Team
Template 5: Safety Incident — Legal-Approved Template
This template is for safety incidents only. It
must be reviewed and approved by Legal before sending
. The placeholders marked [LEGAL] require Legal input — do not fill them in without approval.

Subject: Important Notice — {Your Company} Service Security / Safety Event — {date}

Dear {customer_name} team,

We are writing to inform you of a security / safety event that occurred on
{date} which may have involved your use of the {Your Company} {service_name}.

What we know:
[LEGAL: Insert approved description of what occurred. No speculation.]

Your potential exposure:
[LEGAL: Insert approved statement of what data or outputs may have been affected.]

What we have done:
[LEGAL: Insert approved mitigation summary.]

What you should do:
[LEGAL: Insert any recommended customer actions, e.g. credential rotation, review of outputs.]

Regulatory context:
[LEGAL: Include only if required — e.g. GDPR Art.33 notification, HIPAA breach notice.]

We take this matter very seriously and are committed to your trust and the
security of your data. If you have questions, please contact:
  security@yourcompany.com
  {csm_email} (your Customer Success Manager)

A post-incident report will be shared with you within {[LEGAL: N]} business days.

— {Your Company} Security & Compliance Team

Do not use this template as a shortcut. If Legal has not approved the text, send a holding message only: 'We are investigating a security-related event and will contact you with full details once our review is complete.'
Template 6: SLA Credit Acknowledgement

For enterprise customers with contractual SLAs that the incident breached, include a credit acknowledgement in the resolution email or as a separate follow-up. Legal and Finance must approve the credit amount and terms.

Subject: {Your Company} — SLA Credit for {date} Incident

Dear {customer_name} team,

Following the service disruption on {date}, we have reviewed your contract
and confirmed that the incident duration ({N} minutes) exceeded your contracted
uptime SLA of {SLA_uptime}%.

In accordance with our Service Level Agreement, we will apply a credit of
{credit_amount} to your next billing cycle. This credit has been applied
automatically — no action is required on your part.

If you have questions about the credit or your SLA terms, please contact
{csm_name} at {csm_email}.

We appreciate your partnership and remain committed to meeting our service
commitments.

— {Your Company} Finance & Customer Success Team
Automating Template Selection and Delivery
At scale, selecting and routing the right template manually is error-prone. A customer communications engine can automate this from the incident record:

from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

class IncidentType(str, Enum):
    AVAILABILITY = "availability"
    QUALITY = "quality"
    SAFETY = "safety"
    COST = "cost"

class CustomerTier(str, Enum):
    ENTERPRISE = "enterprise"
    SELF_SERVE = "self_serve"

@dataclass
class CustomerNotificationConfig:
    incident_type: IncidentType
    severity: str
    affected_regions: list[str]
    incident_start: str
    incident_id: str
    ic_name: str
    legal_approved: bool = False  # required for safety

def select_template(config: CustomerNotificationConfig, customer: dict) -> Optional[str]:
    """Return the template key to use, or None if no notification warranted."""
    if not _customer_is_affected(customer, config.affected_regions):
        return None  # status page only

    if config.incident_type == IncidentType.SAFETY:
        if not config.legal_approved:
            return "safety_holding"  # holding message only until Legal approves
        return "safety_approved"

    tier = CustomerTier(customer["tier"])
    if tier == CustomerTier.ENTERPRISE:
        return "enterprise_declaration"
    else:
        return "self_serve_broadcast"

def render_template(template_key: str, config: CustomerNotificationConfig, customer: dict) -> dict:
    """Render a template to subject + body strings."""
    templates = load_templates()  # load from YAML or DB
    tmpl = templates[template_key]
    context = {
        "customer_name": customer["name"],
        "customer_technical_contact": customer["technical_contact"],
        "csm_email": customer.get("csm_email", "support@yourcompany.com"),
        "incident_id": config.incident_id,
        "start_time": config.incident_start,
        "region": ", ".join(config.affected_regions),
        "ic_name": config.ic_name,
        "severity": config.severity,
    }
    return {
        "subject": tmpl["subject"].format(**context),
        "body": tmpl["body"].format(**context),
        "to": customer["technical_contact"],
        "cc": [customer.get("csm_email", ""), "incidents@yourcompany.com"],
    }
GenAI-Specific Communication Considerations
AI outages have characteristics that differ from traditional API outages. Address these in your templates:
Incident type
What customers actually need to know
Template adjustment
LLM hallucination spike
Were outputs they received during the window potentially incorrect?
Advise customers to review and verify outputs generated during the affected window
Safety filter failure
Did any users receive harmful or policy-violating content?
Legal must approve language; advise customers on review steps
Model quality degradation
Was a different model silently served? Were results below expected quality?
Clarify which model version was active; offer re-runs if feasible
Context window corruption
Did any completions use corrupted or wrong context?
Be specific about whether outputs were affected or only latency
RAG retrieval failure
Were AI responses based on stale or wrong retrieved context?
Advise reviewing outputs that relied on retrieval during the window
Rate limit cascade
Did throttling cause some requests to fail silently?
Advise checking for failed requests in the dashboard; offer retry guidance
AI Outage Communications Anti-Patterns
Anti-Pattern
Why it fails for AI outages
Correct approach
Generic 'service disruption' language
AI outages can mean wrong answers, not just no answers — customers need to know if outputs were affected
Be specific: 'some responses may have been lower quality than usual' vs 'requests returned errors'
Saying 'the AI was wrong'
Implies the AI is usually right — sets wrong expectations; legal exposure
Use 'outputs may not have met our quality standards during this window'
Promising 'the AI is fixed'
Models don't get 'fixed' like servers — confusing to customers
Say 'service has been restored to normal operation'
Revealing model names/versions
Can expose competitive information or be used to craft targeted attacks
Use service names only: 'Inference API', not 'GPT-4 turbo'
Not addressing output validity
Customers may have acted on bad AI outputs and need to know
Always state whether outputs during the window should be reviewed
Summary
Use the three-question decision tree: directly impacted? safety incident? enterprise or self-serve?
Enterprise customers get personalised email at T+30 for SEV1; self-serve users get broadcast at resolution
Safety incident templates require Legal review and approval before any message is sent
SLA credit acknowledgements require Legal/Finance approval — do not commit to credits in the initial comms
AI outages differ from API outages: always address whether outputs were affected, not just availability
Never promise 'the AI is fixed' — say 'service has been restored to normal operation'
Automate template selection and rendering from the incident record to ensure consistency under pressure
Post-incident reports within five business days are a customer expectation for SEV1 and SEV2 incidents
